stem: A point is chosen uniformly at random on the segment from \( 0 \) to \( [[L]] \) on the number line. What is the probability that the chosen point lies between \( [[a]] \) and \( [[b]] \)?
solution: For a uniform point, the probability is the favorable length over the total length: \( \frac{[[b]] - [[a]]}{[[L]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the endpoint \( [[b]] \) as the favorable length instead of the sub-interval length \( [[b]] - [[a]] \).
This uses the endpoint \( [[a]] \) instead of the sub-interval length.
This divides by \( [[b]] \) instead of the full length \( [[L]] \).
This adds the endpoints instead of subtracting to get the sub-interval length.
This is the probability the point lies OUTSIDE the sub-interval, the complement.
This divides by \( [[L]] + 1 \) instead of \( [[L]] \).

stem: A dart is thrown at random and lands uniformly on a rectangular board that is \( [[W]] \) inches by \( [[H]] \) inches. A rectangular target region \( [[w]] \) inches by \( [[h]] \) inches is marked inside the board (fully contained within it). What is the probability that the dart lands inside the target region?
solution: For a uniform point in a region, probability equals favorable area over total area: \( \dfrac{[[w]]\cdot[[h]]}{[[W]]\cdot[[H]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses only the width ratio, ignoring the height dimension of the target.
This uses only the height ratio, ignoring the width dimension of the target.
This adds the side lengths (perimeter-like) instead of multiplying them into areas.
This divides the target area by the sum of the board's side lengths instead of its area.
This is the probability the dart lands OUTSIDE the target region, the complement.
This cross-multiplies the dimensions incorrectly instead of computing (target area)/(board area).

stem: Two friends independently arrive at a cafe at uniformly random times somewhere within a [[L]]-minute window. Each is willing to wait [[m]] minutes for the other before leaving. What is the probability that they actually meet (that is, their arrival times differ by at most [[m]] minutes)?
solution: Model each arrival time as a coordinate in an \( [[L]] \times [[L]] \) square. They meet exactly when \( |x-y| \le [[m]] \), the complement of two corner triangles each with legs \( [[L]]-[[m]] \): \( P(\text{meet}) = 1 - \left(\dfrac{[[L]]-[[m]]}{[[L]]}\right)^2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the probability they do NOT meet (the two corner triangles), the complement of the requested event.
This treats the problem as a simple 1-D length ratio, ignoring the 2-D square of possible arrival-time pairs.
This is the complement of the naive 1-D ratio, still ignoring the actual square geometry.
This doubles the 1-D ratio instead of correctly using the squared-area complement.
This takes only the linear (not squared) complement of the ratio \( ([[L]]-[[m]])/[[L]] \).
This squares the 1-D ratio itself rather than its complement.

stem: A spinner needle comes to rest at a uniformly random point around a circular dial marked in degrees from \( 0 \) to \( 360 \). A particular sector of the dial spans [[d]] degrees. What is the probability that the needle stops within that sector?
solution: For a uniformly random angle, probability equals favorable arc measure over the full \( 360^\circ \): \( \dfrac{[[d]]}{360} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio of the sector angle to the full circle.
This is the probability the needle lands OUTSIDE the sector, the complement.
This uses \( 180^\circ \) (a half circle) as the denominator instead of the full \( 360^\circ \).
This uses \( 720^\circ \) (two full circles) as the denominator instead of \( 360^\circ \).
This doubles the sector angle before dividing, overcounting the favorable region.
This is off by one degree from the correct sector measure.
